The Teacher's final word cuts through the fog of empty pursuits like a blade. After wrestling with wisdom, pleasure, labor, and injustice across eleven chapters, we arrive here: Remember your Creator while you still can. Not when your hands shake, your vision blurs, your appetite vanishes, and the fear of falling grips you. Not when your silver hair whitens like the almond tree blossoms and even the smallest grasshopper's leap feels impossible. The poetry of aging here is not sentimental—it is terrifying. These are the years when you find no pleasure in anything, when the house you lived in becomes a trembling prison and the mill grinds silent because there are too few left to turn it. The Teacher is not offering you time. He is telling you time is already running out, and you know it.

This is why youth must remember. Not in some abstract, theoretical way. The young do not yet believe in their own decay. They have not yet felt the sting of irrelevance, the exhaustion of grasping for meaning in work that yields nothing, the disillusionment of discovering that even wisdom cannot stop the wheel from breaking at the well. But the young can choose now—not later, when the body fails and the spirit returns to God who gave it—to orient their lives toward the only One who endures. To remember your Creator in your strength is to stake your existence on something other than your own vitality, achievements, or understanding. It is to admit that you belong to Him, not to yourself.

Yet the Teacher does not abandon us to existential despair dressed up as piety. Verses 9 through 12 remind us that wisdom, though limited, is real. The words of the wise are like goads—they provoke us forward. They are like nails driven by a single Shepherd, fixed and reliable even if nothing else is. Even the Teacher's own labor—arranging proverbs, searching for truth—has meaning because it serves something beyond the moment. But this wisdom cannot be the end in itself. It can point you toward obedience; it cannot save you from judgment. That is why the conclusion rings with such finality: Fear God and keep His commandments, because this is the whole duty of man. Not the whole achievement. Not the whole satisfaction. The whole duty.

Everything else flows from this: the years will come when pleasure fades and strength fails, and you cannot stop them. Your deeds will be brought into judgment—every one, including the ones no one else sees. This is not a threat meant to crush you with fear; it is clarity meant to reorient you. If you are young, remember now. If you are not, it is still not too late to fear the God who sees all and will judge all. Your life has one true purpose: to recognize His lordship and live under it. That is not futile. That is the only thing that lasts.
